statementLines: Consider the following statements with regard to pardoning power of the President of India:
I. The exercise of this power by the President can be subjected to limited judicial review.
II. The President can exercise this power without the advice of the Central Government.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements with regard to pardoning power of the President of India: I. The exercise of this power by the President can be subjected to limited judicial review. II. The President can exercise this power without the advice of the Central Government. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**Under Article 72 of the Indian Constitution, the President of India has the power to grant pardons, reprieves, respites, remissions of punishment, or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of an offence. This includes: 1. Punishment by a Court Martial, 2. Offences under Union law, and 3. Death sentences.**

Statement I is correct. **The**Supreme Court in the Maru Ram v. Union of India (1980) case held that the President's **power under Article 72 is subject to judicial review**. Further, in Shatrughan Chauhan v. Union of India (2014), the court ruled that the pardoning power of the President of India is not absolute. Judicial review can be exercised to ensure that the power is not used arbitrarily, or in bad faith, or based on extraneous considerations.**

Statement II is incorrect.**As per**Article 74 of the Constitution, the President is bound to act on the aid and advice **of the Council of Ministers. **This includes the exercise of clemency powers under Article 72. The President cannot exercise this power independently or without the advice of the government.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following:
I. President of India
II. Vice-President of India
III. Prime Minister and Union Council of Ministers
IV. Attorney General of India
How many of the above form part of the Union Executive as per the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following: I. President of India II. Vice-President of India III. Prime Minister and Union Council of Ministers IV. Attorney General of India How many of the above form part of the Union Executive as per the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. The Union Executive in India is described in**Part V of the Constitution**, specifically in**Articles 52 to 78**. The Union Executive consists of the**President, the Vice-President, the Prime Minister, the Union Council of Ministers, and **the Attorney General of India.** Option I is correct**.**Article 53 states that, **the**executive power of the Union shall be vested in the President**and shall be exercised by him either directly or through officers subordinate to him in accordance with this Constitution. Therefore, the**President is the part of the Union Executive. ** Option II is correct**. The office of the**Vice-President**is established under**Article 63**. The office is ranked second in the order of precedence. According to**Article 64**, the**Vice-President shall be ex officio Chairman**of the Council of the States. The**Vice-President can act as President**or discharge his functions during casual vacancies in the office, or during the absence of the President**(Article 65)**. The**Vice-President is part of the Union Executive**.** Option III is correct**. According to**Article 74**, There shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President.**This makes the Prime Minister and the Council of Ministers the real **executive**, as they wield actual authority. Additionally,**Article 78**specifies that it shall be the** duty of the Prime **Minister to communicate to the President all decisions of the Council of Ministers**relating to the administration of the affairs of the Union and proposals for legislation.** Option IV is correct**. According to**Article 76 (1),**the President shall appoint a person who is qualified to be appointed a Judge of the Supreme Court to be Attorney-General for India.**Article 76 (2)**states that it shall be the**duty of the **Attorney-General to give advice to the Government of India upon such legal matters**, and to perform such other duties of a legal character, as may from time to time be referred or assigned to him by the President, and to discharge the functions conferred on him by or under this Constitution or any other law for the time being in force. The**Attorney General of India is part of the Union Executive**.

statementLines: With reference to removal of the Governor of a state in India, consider the following statements:
I. As per the Constitution, the Governor of a state can only be removed on the ground of violation of the Constitution.

II. The President must remove the Governor if the state legislature passes a resolution to that effect with a majority of total membership of House/Houses and the majority of not less than two-third of members present and voting in House/Houses of the legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to removal of the Governor of a state in India, consider the following statements: I. As per the Constitution, the Governor of a state can only be removed on the ground of violation of the Constitution. II. The President must remove the Governor if the state legislature passes a resolution to that effect with a majority of total membership of House/Houses and the majority of not less than two-third of members present and voting in House/Houses of the legislature. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **As per Art. 156, A Governor shall hold office for a term of five years from the date on which he enters upon his office. However, this term of five years is subject to the pleasure of the President. Further, he can resign at any time by addressing a resignation letter to the President.**

Statement I is incorrect: **The Constitution does not lay down any grounds upon which a governor may be removed by the President.**

Statement II is incorrect: **There is no such provision in the Constitution that the President must remove the Governor if the state legislature passes a resolution to that effect with a majority of total membership of House/Houses and the majority of not less than two-third of members present and voting in House/Houses of the legislature. The state government is not involved in the removal process as per the provisions of the Constitution. However, this has been recommended by the Punchhi Commission (2010) that he or she should be removed only by a resolution of the state legislature.

statementLines: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following situations:
I. To seek information relating to the administration of the affairs of the Union
II. Appointing the Prime minister when no party or coalition has a clear majority in Lok Sabha.
III. Withdrawal of an Ordinance issued under Article 123 of the Constitution of India.
In
how many of the above situations, the President of India can exercise his/her discretionary powers?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following situations: I. To seek information relating to the administration of the affairs of the Union II. Appointing the Prime minister when no party or coalition has a clear majority in Lok Sabha. III. Withdrawal of an Ordinance issued under Article 123 of the Constitution of India. In how many of the above situations, the President of India can exercise his/her discretionary powers?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. The President of India is generally bound by the advice of the Council of Ministers under**Article 74(1)**. However, in certain**specific political and constitutional situations**, the President can act**at his own discretion**.**

Statement I is correct**.Under article 78 of the Constitution of India, the President has the right/power to seek information relating to the administration of the affairs of the Union. This is the discretionary power of the President.**

Statement II is correct**. Article 75(1) states that,**the Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President and the **other Ministers shall be appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister**. When no political party or coalition of parties enjoy the majority in Lok Sabha, then the President has**discretion**in inviting the leader of that party or coalition of parties who in his opinion is able to form government.**

Statement III is incorrect**. According to**Article 123**(1)**,**If at any time, except when both Houses of Parliament are in **session, the President is satisfied that circumstances exist which render it necessary for him to take immediate **action, he may promulgate Ordinances**as the circumstances appear to him to require. However, the**issuance and **withdrawal of an Ordinance**must be**based on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers**, as per**Article 74(1)**. Hence, the**withdrawal of an ordinance is not a discretionary power**. The President cannot act independently here.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=4-10 - Discretionary Powers of the President - Page 85;

statementLines: With reference to the Union Government, consider the following statements:
1. N. Gopala Swamy Iyengar Committee suggested that a minister and a secretary be designated solely for pursuing the subject of administrative reform and promoting it.
2. In 1970, the Department of Personnel was constituted on the recommendation of the Administrative Reforms Commission, 1966, and this was placed under the Prime Minister's charge.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Union Government, consider the following statements: 1. N. Gopala Swamy Iyengar Committee suggested that a minister and a secretary be designated solely for pursuing the subject of administrative reform and promoting it. 2. In 1970, the Department of Personnel was constituted on the recommendation of the Administrative Reforms Commission, 1966, and this was placed under the Prime Minister's charge. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**

Statement 1 is incorrect. **Sh. N. Gopala Swamy Ayyangar, in his Report namely 'Reorganization of the Machinery of Central Government' in 1950 recommended the grouping of ministries, improvement in the capabilities of the personnel, and also in the working of the O&M Division. It**nowhere mentions that a minister and a secretary be **designated solely for pursuing the subject of administrative reform and promoting it. **

Statement 2 is correct. **In 1970, on the basis of the recommendations of the**Administrative Reforms Commission**, the**Department of Personnel was set up in the Cabinet Secretariat.** It was placed under the Prime Minister's Office.

statementLines: Which of the following statements with regard to the Vice President of India is/are correct?
I. S/he is elected with the system of proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote.
II. Nominated members of the Council of States do not form part of the electoral college for the Vice President's election.
III. Even after the expiry of the term, the Vice President continues to hold office until his/her successor enters upon the office.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements with regard to the Vice President of India is/are correct? I. S/he is elected with the system of proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote. II. Nominated members of the Council of States do not form part of the electoral college for the Vice President's election. III. Even after the expiry of the term, the Vice President continues to hold office until his/her successor enters upon the office. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer**. The office of the**Vice-President**is established under**Article 63**. The office is ranked second in the order of precedence. According to**Article 64**, the**Vice-President shall be ex officio Chairman**of the Council of the States.**

Statement I is correct**. As per**Article 66(1)**, the**Vice-President**shall be elected by the [members of an electoral college consisting of the members of both Houses of Parliament] in accordance with the**system of proportional **representation by means of the single transferable vote **and the voting at such election shall be by secret ballot.**

Statement II is incorrect**. Members of an electoral college**consisting of the members of both Houses of Parliament, **including both elected and nominated members.**This is**unlike the election of the President**, where only elected members vote.**

Statement III is correct**.**Article 67(c)**states that a**Vice-President **shall, notwithstanding the expiration of his term,**continue to hold office until his successor enters upon his office**. This means that even after the expiry of the term, the Vice-President continues in office until the successor takes charge, ensuring no constitutional vacancy.

source.sourceText: https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=4-10 - The Vice President of India - Page 88 ; Mastering Indian Polity by Forum IAS - 1st Edition - Chapter 15 - Executive Branch Page 203 to 206 https://www.deccanherald.com/opinion/heres-why-the-vice-presidents-office-an-american-import-has-limited- relevance-in-india-3655007

statementLines: Consider the following:
I. Violation of the Constitution.
II. Engaging in any office of profit under the Government of India.
III. Misuse of discretionary powers.
How many of the above are explicitly mentioned in the Constitution of India as grounds for removal of the President of India by impeachment?
questions.questionText: Consider the following: I. Violation of the Constitution. II. Engaging in any office of profit under the Government of India. III. Misuse of discretionary powers. How many of the above are explicitly mentioned in the Constitution of India as grounds for removal of the President of India by impeachment?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**. The Constitution of India lays down a very specific provision for the**removal of the President**under**Article 61**, which is through a process called**impeachment**. Article 61 states that - (1) When a President is to be impeached**for violation of the Constitution**, the charge shall be preferred by either House of Parliament. (2) No such charge shall be preferred unless - (a) the proposal to prefer such charge is contained in a resolution which has been moved**after at least fourteen days' **notice in writing**signed by not less than**one-fourth of the total number of members **of the House has been given of their intention to move the resolution, and (b) such**resolution has been passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the House**.**Violation of the Constitution **is the**only ground** mentioned in the Constitution for the removal of the President.

questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the office of the Prime Minister of India:
I. A person can be appointed as Prime Minister only after proving majority in the House of the People.
II. The Prime Minister advises the President on the allocation and reshuffling of portfolios among the ministers.
III. The Prime Minister can recommend the dissolution of the House of the People to the President of India before the completion of its five-year term.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the office of the Prime Minister of India: I. A person can be appointed as Prime Minister only after proving majority in the House of the People. II. The Prime Minister advises the President on the allocation and reshuffling of portfolios among the ministers. III. The Prime Minister can recommend the dissolution of the House of the People to the President of India before the completion of its five-year term. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**The Prime Minister (PM) of India is the real executive head of the Union government and occupies a central position in the parliamentary system. As the leader of the Council of Ministers and the House of people, the PM coordinates the functioning of the government, advises the President, and shapes key policies and decisions.**

Statement I is incorrect.**The**Constitution does not require a person to demonstrate majority support prior to **appointment as Prime Minister.**Under**Article 75(1)**, the Prime Minister is appointed by the President, and the other Ministers are appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister. By**constitutional convention, the **President usually appoints the leader of the majority party or coalition in the House of People**. Further, under**Article 75(5)**, a person who is not a member of either House of Parliament may be appointed as Prime Minister, but must become a member of either House within six months.**

Statement II is correct.**According to**Article 75 (1) **of the Constitution of India,** the other Ministers shall be **appointed by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister.**The Prime Minister decides the allocation of portfolios among the ministers and may reallocate, reshuffle, upgrade, or downgrade ministerial portfolios from time to time. As the head of the Council of Ministers, the Prime Minister also has the authority to require a minister to resign or to advise the President to dismiss a minister, if necessary.**

Statement III is correct.**As head of the government, the Prime Minister may**advise the President to dissolve the **House of people**before its five-year term**(Article 85 read with Article 74)**.

source.sourceText: Pg: 90- https://ncert.nic.in/textbook.php?keps2=4-10 Mastering Indian Polity by ForumIAS, 1st edition, Chapter: 15 - Executive Branch, Page-287,288 https://ramnathkovind.nic.in/pdf/pr030917_2.pdf

statementLines: In the context of Indian Polity, consider the following statements:
Statement I: The total number of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, in the Union Council of Ministers shall not exceed fifteen percent of the total number of members of the House of the People.
Statement II: The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 placed limits on the size of the Union Council of Ministers.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: In the context of Indian Polity, consider the following statements: Statement I: The total number of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, in the Union Council of Ministers shall not exceed fifteen percent of the total number of members of the House of the People. Statement II: The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 placed limits on the size of the Union Council of Ministers. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**

Statement I is correct.**The 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003 inserted Article** 75 (1A), **which states that,**The **total number of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, in the Council of Ministers shall not exceed fifteen per **cent. of the total number of members of the House of the People. **This limit was introduced to prevent excessively large ministries, which were common before 2003 and were often used as tools for political bargaining.**

Statement II is correct.**The**91st Amendment Act, 2003**created a**ceiling on the size of the Council of Ministers at **both the Centre and in the States under Article 75 (1A) and 164(1A). **This was done to ensure administrative efficiency and discourage the practice of offering ministerial berths to secure political support.**Statement II is the correct explanation for statement I.**It was **through the 91st Amendment Act** that this 15% limit was introduced on the size of the Council of Ministers at both the Union and State levels to prevent political instability, patronage politics, and unnecessary financial burden on the state.**

questions.microTopic: Prime Minister, Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Cabinet Executive
statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the Union Council of Ministers:
I. If an individual minister does not agree with a policy or decision of the Council of Ministers, he/she must either accept the decision of the Council or resign.
II. If a censure motion is passed in the House of people against any individual minister, the Council of Ministers have to resign.
III. The death of the incumbent Prime Minister automatically leads to the resignation of the Council of Ministers. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the Union Council of Ministers: I. If an individual minister does not agree with a policy or decision of the Council of Ministers, he/she must either accept the decision of the Council or resign. II. If a censure motion is passed in the House of people against any individual minister, the Council of Ministers have to resign. III. The death of the incumbent Prime Minister automatically leads to the resignation of the Council of Ministers. Which of the following statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.**Article 75 (3) states that the Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the House of the People.**It is a foundational principle of the parliamentary system adopted by India. It ensures that the Council of Ministers acts as a unified body accountable to the House of People.**

Statement I is correct.**Collective responsibility requires that all ministers publicly uphold Council of **Ministers/Cabinet decisions**, regardless of personal disagreement.** A minister who cannot support a Council of **Ministers/Cabinet decision must resign, as public dissent undermines the unity and credibility of the government**. This principle ensures that the executive functions with a single voice. For**example, Union Minister Harsimrat Kaur **Badal resigned in 2020 **after opposing the Cabinet-approved farm laws, in conformity with the doctrine of collective responsibility.**

Statement II is incorrect.**A**Censure Motion**is moved against the Government to censure its policy in a particular direction or an individual minister or ministers. It is moved to censure the Council of Ministers for specific policies and actions. If**it is passed in the House of People, the Council of Ministers need not resign from office. **

Statement III is correct.**The Prime Minister is the head of the Union Council of Ministers and its central unifying authority. The Union Council of Ministers**cannot exist without the Prime Minister**. Therefore, the**death or **resignation of the Prime Minister automatically dissolves the entire Council of Ministers.**

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following statements:
I. The Governor of a State is not answerable to any court for the exercise and performance of the powers and duties of his/her office.
II. No criminal proceedings shall be instituted or continued against the Governor during his/her term of office.
III. Members of a State Legislature are not liable to any proceedings in any court in respect of anything said within the House.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following statements: I. The Governor of a State is not answerable to any court for the exercise and performance of the powers and duties of his/her office. II. No criminal proceedings shall be instituted or continued against the Governor during his/her term of office. III. Members of a State Legislature are not liable to any proceedings in any court in respect of anything said within the House. Which of the statements given above are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct.**Article 361 of Indian Constitution provides that the President, or the Governor of a State, **shall not be answerable to any court for the exercise and performance of the powers and duties of his offic**e or for any act done or purporting to be done by him in the exercise and performance of those powers and duties.**

Statement II is correct.**Article 361 (2) says that no criminal proceedings shall be instituted or continued against **the President**, or the Governor of a State, in any court during his term of office.**

Statement III is correct.**The**Constitution of India provides immunity to a member of Parliament/State **Legislature from any proceedings in any court** in respect of anything said or any vote given by him in the legislature or any committee thereof. Article 194 of the Constitution says that "No member of the Legislature of a State shall be liable to any proceedings in any court in respect of anything said or any vote given by him in the Legislature or any committee thereof."

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: With reference to the Presidential Election in India, consider the following statements:
I. Each elected member of either House of Parliament shall have equal value of vote.
II. The value of vote of a Member of the Legislative Assembly is uniform across all the States.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Presidential Election in India, consider the following statements: I. Each elected member of either House of Parliament shall have equal value of vote. II. The value of vote of a Member of the Legislative Assembly is uniform across all the States. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer.**

Statement I is correct.**Article 55(2)(c) **states that,**each elected member of either House of Parliament shall have **such number of votes as may be obtained by dividing the total number of votes assigned to the members of the **Legislative Assemblies of the States by the total number of the elected members of both Houses of Parliament. **Accordingly, the Constitution provides that every elected Member of Parliament, whether from the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha, has an equal value of vote.**

Statement II is incorrect.**According to**Article 55(2)(a),**every elected member of the Legislative Assembly of a State shall have as many votes as there are multiples of one thousand in the quotient obtained by dividing the population of the State by the total number of the elected members of the Assembly. Consequently, the value of a Member of the Legislative Assembly's vote is**not uniform across all States**. Since different States have different populations and different numbers of MLAs, the value of an MLA's vote varies from State to State. For instance, an MLA from**Uttar Pradesh(208)**has a significantly higher vote value than an MLA from**Sikkim(7)**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding the Solicitor General of India (SGI):
I. The Solicitor General of India's office is a constitutional office created under Article 76 of the Constitution.
II. S/he is appointed by the President of India by warrant under his/her hand and seal.
III. Like the Attorney General of India, s/he has the right to participate in the proceedings of Parliament.
Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding the Solicitor General of India (SGI): I. The Solicitor General of India's office is a constitutional office created under Article 76 of the Constitution. II. S/he is appointed by the President of India by warrant under his/her hand and seal. III. Like the Attorney General of India, s/he has the right to participate in the proceedings of Parliament. Which of the above statements is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.**The Solicitor General of India (SGI) holds the position of the country's second-highest law officer, supporting the **Attorney General for India.**By assisting in legal matters and providing expert opinions on complex legal issues, the SGI ensures the efficient functioning of the government's legal framework.**

Statement I is incorrect: Article 76**of the Constitution**provides only for the Attorney General of India**(AGI). The**Solicitor General of India**(SGI) and**Additional Solicitors General**(ASGs) are**not constitutional posts.**SGI is a statutory position**appointed to assist the Attorney General in performing duties related to legal affairs of the Union Government. The office of SGI and its responsibilities are governed by the**Law Officers (Conditions of Service) Rules, 1987**, not by the Constitution.**

Statement II is incorrect: **The**Attorney General of India is appointed by the President under Article 76.**The**SGI is **appointed by the Appointments Committee of the Cabinet**(ACC) (chaired by the Prime Minister of India) generally for a**term of three years**. The**appointment does not involve a Presidential warrant under his/her hand and seal**. The SGI is a contractual government law officer, serving at the pleasure of the Government of India.**

Statement III is incorrect: Only **the**Attorney General of India is granted **the** right to participate in parliamentary **proceedings under Article 88**. The**Solicitor General,**being a statutory officer,**has no right to participate in the proceedings of Parliament. **Knowledge Base:**Additional Key Points on the Solicitor General of India (SGI)**Duties: **Appears in courts on behalf of the**Government of India**in important legal matters. Represents the government in**presidential references**to the Supreme Court under**Article 143**. Performs**other legal duties**assigned by the Central Government.**Restrictions on Private Practice **Cannot hold briefs for**any party other than the government**. Cannot**defend accused persons**without prior government approval. Cannot accept**any office**or appointment without government permission. Cannot provide**legal advice**to any party**against the Government of India**or**public sector undertakings (PSUs)**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part VIII & X: Union Territories and Scheduled Areas
statementLines: Under the provisions of the Fifth Schedule of the Constitution of India, which of the following is empowered to direct that any area or part thereof shall cease to be a Scheduled Area?
questions.questionText: Under the provisions of the Fifth Schedule of the Constitution of India, which of the following is empowered to direct that any area or part thereof shall cease to be a Scheduled Area?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Under the provisions of the**Fifth Schedule**of the Constitution, the**President of India has the exclusive authority to: **declare any area to be a Scheduled Area, increase or decrease its size, alter its boundaries, and**direct that whole or any specified part of a Scheduled Area shall cease to be a Scheduled Area **or a part of such an area. The Governor has advisory and reporting functions regarding Scheduled Areas but does not have the power to alter their status. Parliament and the Ministry of Tribal Affairs also have no constitutional role in this specific declaration. Knowledge Base:**Key Points about Fifth Schedule
- **Applies to:**Administration and control of**Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes**in**10 states**Objective:**Ensure socio-economic protection, self-governance, land security, and cultural preservation of tribal communities.**Definition & alteration of Scheduled Areas:**Done**only by the President**.**Role of Governor:**Submits**annual or special reports**to the President**regarding administration of Scheduled Areas and may make regulations to control land transfer, money-lending, etc.,**with Presidential assent**.**Tribes Advisory Council (TAC):**To be constituted in each state having Scheduled Areas;**three-fourths of **members must be ST representatives** from the Legislative Assembly; advises on welfare and development matters.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
statementLines: Consider the following statements:

1. If the election of the President of India is declared void by the Supreme Court of India, all acts done by him/her in the performance of duties of his/her office of President before the date of decision become invalid.
2. Election for the post of the President of India can be postponed on the ground that some Legislative Assemblies have been dissolved, and elections are yet to take place.
3. When a Bill is presented to the President of India, the Constitution prescribes time limits he/she has to declare his/her assent.
How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: 1. If the election of the President of India is declared void by the Supreme Court of India, all acts done by him/her in the performance of duties of his/her office of President before the date of decision become invalid. 2. Election for the post of the President of India can be postponed on the ground that some Legislative Assemblies have been dissolved, and elections are yet to take place. 3. When a Bill is presented to the President of India, the Constitution prescribes time limits he/she has to declare his/her assent. How many of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**The President is elected indirectly by the electoral college consisting of elected members of both the houses of Parliament, elected members of State legislative Assembly and elected members of the legislative assemblies of the Union Territories of Delhi and Puducherry.**

Statement 1 is incorrect: **If the election of a person as President is**declared void by the Supreme Court**, acts done by him before the date of such declaration of the Supreme Court are**not invalidated and continue to remain in force.**

Statement 2 is incorrect: **The**Supreme court in Presidential Poll vs Unknown case of 1974 **held that the**dissolution **of state legislative assembly will not be a ground for preventing the holding of the election on the expiry of the **term of the President**. Nor can it be a ground to suggest that the election to office of the President could be held only after the election to the state is held, where the Legislative Assembly of a State is dissolved.**

Statement 3 is incorrect: **The Constitution of India does**not prescribe any time-limit **within which the President has to take decision with respect to a bill presented to him/her for his/her assent. Thus, the**President of India can **simply keep the bills pending for an indefinite period.**

statementLines: With reference to Constitution of India, consider the following statements:
Statement I: The scope of the pardoning power of the President of India under Article 72 of the Constitution of India is wider than the pardoning power of the Governor of a state under Article 161.
Statement II: The President has the power to grant pardons in all cases where the punishment or sentence is by a Court Martial, whereas the Governor of state does not possess any such power.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to Constitution of India, consider the following statements: Statement I: The scope of the pardoning power of the President of India under Article 72 of the Constitution of India is wider than the pardoning power of the Governor of a state under Article 161. Statement II: The President has the power to grant pardons in all cases where the punishment or sentence is by a Court Martial, whereas the Governor of state does not possess any such power. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct:**The**Constitution of India provides for pardoning powers**under**Article 72 for the President **of India**and under**Article 161 for the Governor of a State**. The President's powers extend to all offences, including those under Central laws and those awarded by Court Martial, while the Governor's authority is limited only to matters within the executive jurisdiction of the State. Therefore, the**scope and jurisdiction of the President's pardoning power is broader than those of the Governor. **

Statement II is correct and explains statement I:**Under Article 72 (1) of the Constitution of India The President shall have the power to grant** pardons,**reprieves, respites or remissions of punishment or to suspend, remit or commute the sentence of any person convicted of any offence - (a)** in all cases where the punishment or sentence is by a **Court Martial; **(b) in all cases where the punishment or sentence is for an offence against any law relating to a matter to which the executive power of the Union extends; (c) in all cases where the sentence is a sentence of death. In contrast,**Article 161 does not grant such power to the Governor**, as the Governor's jurisdiction does not extend to military law or military courts. Hence,**this explains why the President's power is wider **(as stated in Statement I).

statementLines: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following statements:
Statement I: In the event of the occurrence of any vacancy in the office of the President by reason of his/her death, the Vice-President shall act as President for a period not exceeding six months.
Statement II: The functions of the office of the Vice-President of India is modelled on the lines of the office of the Vice-President of the United States of America.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Indian polity, consider the following statements: Statement I: In the event of the occurrence of any vacancy in the office of the President by reason of his/her death, the Vice-President shall act as President for a period not exceeding six months. Statement II: The functions of the office of the Vice-President of India is modelled on the lines of the office of the Vice-President of the United States of America. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **The**Vice-President of India**is the**second-highest constitutional office**in the country, created under**Article 63**of the Constitution. The primary function of the Vice-President is to**act as the ex-officio Chairman of the Rajya Sabha**, ensuring the smooth conduct of parliamentary proceedings in the Upper House. The Vice-President is elected indirectly by an electoral college consisting of members of both Houses of Parliament, serving a term of five years.**

Statement I is correct: **Article 65(1) of the Constitution states that the**Vice-President shall act as President in the **event of a vacancy in the office of the President**due to death, resignation, removal, or otherwise. Further, Article 62(1) mandates that an election to fill the vacancy must be held within six months. Thus, the**Vice-President acts as President only until a new President is elected**, and**this period cannot exceed six **months. **

Statement II is correct: **The**functions of the office of the Vice-President of India is modelled on the lines of **functions of the office of the Vice-President of the United States of America**,as both hold the ex-officio position of presiding officer of the Upper House of the Parliament i.e. Rajya Sabha in case of India and the Senate in case of the United States of America (U.S.A).**Statement II does not explain Statement I: **Even though the office of Vice President of India is modelled based on the U.S. system, there is an**important **constitutional difference**: In the**United States**, when the presidency falls vacant, the**Vice-President automatically succeeds and continues **as President for the remainder of the term**. In**India**, the**Vice-President does not succeed to the presidency**for the remaining term. Instead, he or she**acts **only as President until a new President is elected**, and that acting period**cannot exceed six months**. Thus, while both statements are individually correct, the**reason behind the six-month limit**in Statement I is**not **connected**to the American model mentioned in Statement II. Hence,**Statement II does not explain Statement I**.

statementLines: Consider the following bodies/organisations in India:
I. National Medical Commission
II. National Security Council
III. Inter-State Council
IV. National Disaster Management Authority
V. Zonal Councils The Prime Minister of India acts as the Chairman of
how many of the above bodies/organisations?
questions.questionText: Consider the following bodies/organisations in India: I. National Medical Commission II. National Security Council III. Inter-State Council IV. National Disaster Management Authority V. Zonal Councils The Prime Minister of India acts as the Chairman of how many of the above bodies/organisations?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. ** Option I is incorrect. The National Medical Commission **consists of 33 members, including the Chairperson, 10 ex-officio members and 22 part-time members. Its**Chairperson is appointed by the Union Government. **It lays down policies for medical education and ensures coordination among the four autonomous boards. The NMC has four separate autonomous boards: under-graduate medical education, post-graduate medical education, medical assessment and rating and ethics and medical registration. The National Medical Commission (NMC) is a statutory body constituted under the National Medical Commission Act, 2019. It has replaced the Medical Council of India.** Option II is correct. The National Security Council (NSC) is headed by the Prime Minister as the Chairman.**It also comprises Minister of Defence, Minister of Home Affairs, Minister of External Affairs and Minister of Finance. The National Security Council advises the Central Government on matters relating to internal security, economic security, technological strength and foreign policy with a view to promote national security goals and objectives. The National Security Adviser acts as the Secretary of the National Security Council.** Option III is correct. The Inter-State Council **was established under Article 263 of the Constitution. It works for Centre-State and interstate coordination and cooperation.**The Prime Minister is the chairman of the Council. Its **other**members include the Chief Ministers of all states and UTs with legislative assemblies, and Administrators of other UTs. Six Ministers of Cabinet rank in the Centre's Council of Ministers, nominated by the Prime Minister, are also its members.** Option IV is correct. **The**National Disaster Management Authority (NDMA)**is India's apex statutory body for disaster management. The NDMA was formally constituted on 27th September 2006, by the Disaster Management Act, 2005.**The Prime Minister is its chairperson **and it has nine other members. One of the nine members is designated as Vice-Chairperson. The NDMA is mandated to lay down the policies, plans and guidelines for Disaster Management to ensure a timely and effective response to disasters.** Option V is incorrect. Zonal Councils **are statutory bodies that were set up under the State Reorganisation Act, 1956. Five Zonal Councils have been set up to develop the habit of cooperative working among the States.**The Union Home **Minister is the chairman of each of these councils. **Organizational structure of Zonal Councils **(i)**Chairman:**The Union Home Minister is the Chairman of each of these Councils. (ii)**Vice Chairman:**The Chief Ministers of the States included in each zone act as Vice-Chairman of the Zonal Council for that zone by rotation, each holding office for a period of one year at a time. (iii)**Members:**Chief Minister and two other Ministers as nominated by the Governor from each of the States and two members from Union Territories included in the zone. (iv)**Advisers:** One officer nominated by the Planning Commission for each of the Zonal Councils, Chief Secretaries and another officer/Development Commissioner nominated by each of the States included in the Zone;

statementLines: With reference to Indian polity, consider the following statements:
Statement I: The Constitution of India does not establish a system of legal responsibility for the individual Ministers of the Union Council of Ministers.
Statement II: The advice given by the Union Council of Ministers to the President of India cannot be questioned in any Court.
Statement III: In India, the individual ministers do not countersign any order of the President of India for a public act.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian polity, consider the following statements: Statement I: The Constitution of India does not establish a system of legal responsibility for the individual Ministers of the Union Council of Ministers. Statement II: The advice given by the Union Council of Ministers to the President of India cannot be questioned in any Court. Statement III: In India, the individual ministers do not countersign any order of the President of India for a public act. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct and both of them explain Statement I
options.b: Both Statement II and Statement III are correct but only one of them explains Statement I
options.c: Only one of the Statements II and III is correct and that explains Statement I
options.d: Neither Statement II nor Statement III is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Statement I and III are correct and statement III explains statement I: **The Constitution of India does not establish a system of legal responsibility of the individual ministers of the Union Council of Ministers. Rather, Article 75 (3) of the Constitution of India states that, the Union Council of Ministers shall be collectively responsible to the House of the People. The Constitution does not require that an order of the President for a public act be countersigned by a minister. This means that Indian ministers are not legally accountable for their official actions in the court of law. Hence Statement III explains statement I. On the contrary, if we see in Britain, every order of the King for a public act is countersigned by a minister. This means that if the order is in violation of any law, the minister would be held responsible and liable in court.**

Statement II is correct. **Article 74 of the Constitution says that there shall be a Council of Ministers to aid and advise the President in the exercise of his functions. It also says that**any advice tendered by Ministers to the President **shall not be inquired into in any court.**Thus, it cannot be questioned in a Court of law regarding** whether any advice **has been tendered to the President or what advice has been tendered. **This provision emphasizes the intimate and confidential relationship between the President and the Union Council of Ministers. But the statement does not directly explain that how and why the Constitution of India does not establish a system of legal responsibility for the individual Ministers of the Union Council of Ministers,

statementLines: With reference to the ordinance-making powers of the Governor of a State, consider the following statements:
I. The Governor's ordinance making power does not extend to Union List subjects of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India under any circumstances.

II. The Governor can promulgate an Ordinance without obtaining instructions from the President, on a matter for which a Bill would have required the previous sanction of the President to be introduced in the state legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the ordinance-making powers of the Governor of a State, consider the following statements: I. The Governor's ordinance making power does not extend to Union List subjects of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India under any circumstances. II. The Governor can promulgate an Ordinance without obtaining instructions from the President, on a matter for which a Bill would have required the previous sanction of the President to be introduced in the state legislature. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The**Constitution empowers both the President (Article 123)**and**the Governor (Article 213) to issue ordinances**when the respective legislatures are not in session and immediate legislative action is required. Ordinances have the same force and effect as laws made by Parliament or the State Legislature, but they are temporary measures,**requiring approval**of the concerned legislature**within six weeks of its reassembly**.**

Statement I is correct: Under Article 213, the Governor may issue ordinances only on subjects on which the State **Legislature has the power to make laws i.e. the State List and Concurrent List subjects under the Seventh Schedule **of the Constitution of India. **There is no provision that expands the Governor's ordinance making power to Union List subjects of the Seventh Schedule of the Constitution of India under any circumstances.**

Statement II is incorrect: Article 213 of the Constitution of India **restricts the Governor's ordinance-making power in three situations.**The Constitution states that the Governor shall not, without instructions from the President, promulgate any **Ordinance if
- **(a) a Bill containing the same provisions would under this Constitution have required the previous sanction of the President for the introduction thereof into the Legislature; or (b) he would have deemed it necessary to reserve a Bill containing the same provisions for the consideration of the President; or (c) an Act of the Legislature of the State containing the same provisions would under this Constitution have been invalid unless, having been reserved for the consideration of the President, it had received the assent of the President. Therefore,**whenever a subject requires the President's prior sanction, the Governor must first obtain the **President's instructions before issuing an Ordinance.**Hence, the Governor has no independent authority in such cases.

statementLines: Consider the following statements :
I. The Constitution of India explicitly mentions that in certain spheres the Governor of a State acts in his/her own discretion.
II. The President of India can, of his/her own, reserve a bill passed by a State Legislature for his/her consideration without it being forwarded by the Governor of the State concerned.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements : I. The Constitution of India explicitly mentions that in certain spheres the Governor of a State acts in his/her own discretion. II. The President of India can, of his/her own, reserve a bill passed by a State Legislature for his/her consideration without it being forwarded by the Governor of the State concerned. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **

Statement I is correct: The Constitution of India explicitly mentions that in certain matters, the Governor acts in **his/ her discretion under Article 163. A**rticle 163(1) of the Indian Constitution states that the Governor is to act on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers, except in matters where the Constitution requires him to act in his discretion. Furthermore, Article 163(2) clarifies that if any question arises regarding whether a matter falls within the Governor's discretionary powers, the Governor's decision shall be final, and the validity of any action taken by the Governor cannot be challenged on the grounds that he should or should not have exercised his discretion.**

Statement II is incorrect:**According to Article 200 of Indian constitution, the Governor has the power to reserve certain bills passed by the State Legislature for the President's consideration.**The President acts only when a bill is **forwarded to him by the Governor and cannot reserve a state bill for consideration on his own. **

statementLines: The Constitution of India describes the Union Cabinet as "the Council consisting of the Prime Minister and other Ministers of Cabinet rank appointed under article 75". In this context, consider the following statements:
I. The term 'Cabinet' is mentioned in Part V of the Constitution of India.
II. The term 'Cabinet' was added to the Indian Constitution through the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1978.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: The Constitution of India describes the Union Cabinet as "the Council consisting of the Prime Minister and other Ministers of Cabinet rank appointed under article 75". In this context, consider the following statements: I. The term 'Cabinet' is mentioned in Part V of the Constitution of India. II. The term 'Cabinet' was added to the Indian Constitution through the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1978. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The Council of Ministers in India is a body that includes all ministers, including Cabinet Ministers, Ministers of State, and Deputy Ministers. Cabinet Ministers are the senior ministers who hold important portfolios such as defence, home affairs, education, and finance etc. The Cabinet is the chief policy formulating body of the Central Government.**

Statement I is incorrect. The word "Cabinet" is mentioned only once in the Indian Constitution in Article 352(3) of **Part XVIII. **Part XVIII is related to Emergency Provisions. Article 352(3) describes the Union Cabinet as the Council consisting of the Prime Minister and other Ministers of Cabinet rank appointed under article 75. Article 352 (3) says that "The President shall not issue a Proclamation under clause (1) or a Proclamation varying such Proclamation unless the decision of the Union Cabinet (that is to say, the Council consisting of the Prime Minister and other Ministers of Cabinet rank appointed under article 75) that such a Proclamation may be issued has been communicated to him in writing."**

Statement II is correct. **Article 352 (3), which mentions the word 'Cabinet', was** added by the 44th Constitutional **Amendment of 1978. **Thus, the term "Cabinet" was added to the Indian Constitution through the 44th Amendment, 1978. Prior to this Amendment, the Constitution did not mention the word 'Cabinet'.

statementLines: Consider the following types of bills passed by both the Houses of the Indian Parliament and presented to the President of India:
I. Ordinary Bills
II. Money Bills
III. Constitutional Amendment Bills under Article 368 of the Constitution of India. For
how many of the above types of bills, the President of India can use suspensive veto?
questions.questionText: Consider the following types of bills passed by both the Houses of the Indian Parliament and presented to the President of India: I. Ordinary Bills II. Money Bills III. Constitutional Amendment Bills under Article 368 of the Constitution of India. For how many of the above types of bills, the President of India can use suspensive veto?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. ** Option 1 is correct and Option II is incorrect: **The power of the President to return the bill to the Parliament for reconsideration is called suspensive veto. President uses his suspensive veto when he returns the bill to the Indian Parliament for its reconsideration. His suspensive veto can be over-ridden by the re-passage of the bill by the Indian Parliament . If the Parliament resends the bill with or without amendment to the Indian President, he has to approve the bill without using any of his veto powers.**As per Article 111 of the Constitution of India, **When a Bill has been passed by the Houses of Parliament, it shall be presented to the President, and the President shall declare either that he assents to the Bill, or that he withholds assent therefrom.**Provided that the **President may, as soon as possible after the presentation to him of a Bill for assent, return the Bill if it is not a Money Bill to the Houses**with a message requesting that they will reconsider the Bill or any specified provisions thereof and, in particular, will consider the desirability of introducing any such amendments as he may recommend in his message, and when a Bill is so returned, the Houses shall reconsider the Bill accordingly, and if the Bill is passed again by the Houses with or without amendment and presented to the President for assent, the President shall not withhold assent therefrom.
**Hence the President of India can return an Ordinary Bill for reconsideration of the Houses (i.e. can use the Suspensive Veto)**but not a Money Bill. ** Option III is incorrect: **Under Article 368, the President has no veto power in respect of Constitutional Amendment Bills. He must give assent once they are duly passed by the Houses of Parliament, and hence cannot exercise suspensive veto on such Bills.

statementLines: With reference to Indian polity, consider the following statements regarding the Cabinet Committees of the Union:
I. They are constitutional bodies established under Article 75 of the Constitution of India.
II. They are constituted by the President of India on the recommendation of the Council of Ministers.
III. At present, there are eight Standing Committees of Cabinet functioning in India.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to Indian polity, consider the following statements regarding the Cabinet Committees of the Union: I. They are constitutional bodies established under Article 75 of the Constitution of India. II. They are constituted by the President of India on the recommendation of the Council of Ministers. III. At present, there are eight Standing Committees of Cabinet functioning in India. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The cabinet makes use of the committee system to facilitate decision-making in specific areas. The main purpose of Cabinet Committees is to reduce the Cabinet's workload by facilitating in depth examination of policy issues and effective coordination.**

Statement I is incorrect: **The**Cabinet Committees are extra constitutional bodies**, established by the Rules of Business, specifically the**Government of India (Transaction of Business) Rules, 1961**. These rules are derived from**Article 77(3)**of the Constitution, which grants the President the power to make rules for the more convenient transaction of the business of the Government of India and for the allocation of business among ministers. 

Statement II is incorrect: Cabinet Committees are constituted by the Prime Minister (not by the President of **India) **according to the needs of the situation. 

Statement III is correct: **Cabinet Committees are of two types-Standing, which are permanent, and Ad-hoc, which are temporary and issue-specific,** There are eight Standing Committees of Cabinet currently functioning at present **namely **the Appointments Committee of the Cabinet, Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs, Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs, Cabinet Committee on Investment and Growth, Cabinet Committee on Security, Cabinet Committee on Parliamentary Affairs, Cabinet Committee on Employment & Skill Development, and Cabinet Committee on Accommodation.

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer **As per Article 160 of the Indian Constitution, the President can make such provision as he thinks fit for the **discharge of the functions of the governor in any contingency not provided in the Constitution. **Example-The death of a sitting governor, the chief justice of the concerned High Court may be appointed temporarily to discharge the functions of the governor of that state.

statementLines: Which of the following statements is not correct about the Attorney General of India?
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is not correct about the Attorney General of India?

options.a: To be Attorney General of India, s/he must be qualified to be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court.
options.b: S/he is appointed for a fixed tenure of five years.
options.c: S/he is not debarred from private legal practice during his/her tenure.
options.d: S/he cannot defend accused persons in criminal prosecutions without the permission of the Government of India.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Attorney-General for India (AG) is the first law officer of the country. She/he advises the central government on legal matters and can participate in parliamentary proceedings, without the right to vote.**

Option a is correct: **As per Article 76(1) of Indian Constitution,** the Attorney General of India must be qualified to be **appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court. **It means he must be a citizen of India and must have been a judge of some high court for five years or an advocate of some high court for ten years or an eminent jurist, in the opinion of the President. 

Option b is incorrect: **Article** 76(4) **of Indian Constitution states that,**the Attorney-General shall hold office during **the pleasure of the President**, and shall receive such remuneration as the President may determine. Generally, the tenure continues until the government that made the appointment completes its term or is replaced. S/he is not appointed for a fixed tenure of five years.**

Option c is correct:**Unlike regular government servants, the Attorney General is permitted to engage in private **legal practice because he/she is not a full-time government servant. **This flexibility enables them to retain their professional skills and continue representing private clients.**

Option d is correct: **Although the Attorney General holds a position of high authority and privilege, certain restrictions are imposed to safeguard impartiality and prevent conflicts of interest.**Accordingly, the Attorney **General cannot appear for accused persons in criminal cases without obtaining prior permission from the **Government of India. **

statementLines: With reference to the Governor of a State in India, consider the following statements:
Statement I: The office of the Governor of a state is an employment under the Government of India.
Statement II: The Governor of a state shall hold office during the pleasure of the President of India.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: With reference to the Governor of a State in India, consider the following statements: Statement I: The office of the Governor of a state is an employment under the Government of India. Statement II: The Governor of a state shall hold office during the pleasure of the President of India. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct, and Statement II explains Statement I
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I
options.c: Statement I is correct, but Statement II is not correct
options.d: Statement I is not correct, but Statement II is correct

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement I is incorrect: **The Supreme Court, in the case of**Hargovind Pant v. Dr. Raghukul Tilak, 1979**observed that it is no doubt true that the Governor is appointed by the President, which means, in effect and substance, the Government of India. However, the Court ruled that from the enumeration of the constitutional powers and functions of the Governor, it is clear that the**Governor is not an employee or a servant of the Government of India**. The Governor is a constitutional functionary.**

Statement II is correct: Article 156(1) **states that** the Governor shall hold office during the pleasure of the President.**

statementLines: Which of the following is/are the qualifications mentioned in the Constitution of India for appointment as the Governor of a State? A person to be eligible for appointment as Governor shall be a citizen of India and-
I. must have completed the age of 35 years.
II. must not be a resident of the State to which s/he is appointed as Governor.
III. must be qualified to be elected as a member of the State Legislative Assembly.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following is/are the qualifications mentioned in the Constitution of India for appointment as the Governor of a State? A person to be eligible for appointment as Governor shall be a citizen of India and- I. must have completed the age of 35 years. II. must not be a resident of the State to which s/he is appointed as Governor. III. must be qualified to be elected as a member of the State Legislative Assembly. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **The qualifications for appointment as a Governor are expressly laid down in**Article 157**of the Constitution of India. According to this Article, a person must satisfy only two conditions:**1. He/She must be a citizen of India. **2. He/She must have completed the age of 35 years. Thus, Option I is correct. **These are the only qualifications mentioned in the Constitution.** Option II is incorrect: The person must be an outsider, i.e., not a resident of the State **This is** not a constitutional requirement. **It is only a political convention followed to ensure impartiality. The Constitution does not mandate that the Governor must be from outside the state.**The Sarkaria Commission also recommended**that the**Governor should be an outsider**to the state, to maintain neutrality and avoid local political pressures.** Option III is incorrect: Qualified to be elected as a member of the State Legislative Assembly **This is** not mentioned in the Constitution. ** Unlike the President and Vice-President (who must be eligible to be members of the Lok Sabha and the Rajya Sabha respectively), the Governor has no such constitutional requirement.

III. Direct the Governor of the state to return the Bill to the State Legislature for reconsideration
How many of the above options are available to the President in such a situation as per the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: A money bill passed by the State legislature is reserved by the Governor for the consideration of the President. In this context consider the following: I. Give assent to the Bill II. Withhold assent to the Bill III. Direct the Governor of the state to return the Bill to the State Legislature for reconsideration How many of the above options are available to the President in such a situation as per the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Under**Article 200**of the Indian Constitution, when a Money Bill is passed by the State Legislature and presented to the Governor, the Governor has the following options: a)**Assent to the Bill**: The Governor may approve the Bill, after which it becomes an act. b)**Withhold Assent**: The Governor may refuse to give assent, and the Bill fails. c)**Reserve the Bill for the Consideration of the President**: The Governor may reserve a Money Bill for the President, especially if the Bill appears to be unconstitutional or affects the powers of the Union Government.**President's Powers when a State Money Bill is Reserved by the Governor for his/her consideration: **When the Governor reserves a State Money Bill for the consideration of the President,**Article 201**applies. Under this Article, the**President has only two options: **a)** Give Assent**: The President may approve the Bill. Once assent is given, the Bill becomes an Act.**Hence, Option I is **correct. **b)**Withhold Assent: **The President may withhold assent. In such a case, the Bill fails.**Hence, Option II is correct. **What the President Cannot Do **The**President cannot return a State Money Bill to the State Legislature for reconsideration**. This power is available only for Bills other than Money Bills.**Hence, Option III is incorrect.**

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.**Statements 1 and 2 are incorrect.**The**Chief Secretary of the State is appointed by the Chief Minister of the State. **There is no fixed tenure of a Chief Secretary in a state**. However, at the central level, there is an assured tenure of two years for Cabinet Secretary, Home Secretary, Defence Secretary, Foreign Secretary as well as RAW and IB chief.
The Administrative Reform Commission in 1969 had recommended that a Chief secretary should have a minimum tenure of three to four years.

statementLines: Which of the following Constitutional Amendment Acts for the first time made it mandatory for the President of India to act on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers?
questions.questionText: Which of the following Constitutional Amendment Acts for the first time made it mandatory for the President of India to act on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Under the**original Constitution**(1950),**Article 74(1)**stated that there "shall be a Council of Ministers to aid and advise the President", but it did not clarify whether the President was bound by this advice. This left a theoretical scope, though not intended in practice, for Presidential discretion.**What the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976 did: **The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1976, made the first explicit and mandatory change by amending Article 74(1) to state that: "There shall be a Council of Ministers with the Prime Minister at the head to aid and advise the President**who **shall, in the exercise of his functions, act in accordance with such advice." **This change removed any ambiguity and**made the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers binding on the **President for the first time**in constitutional history. Thus, the**42nd Amendment is the amendment that first made it mandatory for the President to act on ministerial **advice**. Knowledge Base:**What did the 44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978 changed?**The**44th Constitutional Amendment Act, 1978**retained the binding nature of advice but introduced a**single, crucial **modification**: It allowed the President to:**1. Once**return the advice of the Council of Ministers for**reconsideration**.**2. But after reconsideration**, if the Council of Ministers**reiterates**the advice, the President is**constitutionally bound**to accept it. [the President may require the Council of Ministers to reconsider such advice, either generally or otherwise, and the President shall act in accordance with the advice tendered after such reconsideration]. Thus, the 44th Amendment restored a**limited discretionary window**while keeping the**mandatory nature** of the advice intact.

statementLines: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the President of India?
I. No civil proceedings in which relief is claimed against the President can be instituted during his/her term of office in any court.
II. No process for the arrest or imprisonment of the President, shall issue from any court during his/her term of office.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is/are correct regarding the President of India? I. No civil proceedings in which relief is claimed against the President can be instituted during his/her term of office in any court. II. No process for the arrest or imprisonment of the President, shall issue from any court during his/her term of office. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.**To ensure the smooth functioning of the constitutional head of State, the President of India enjoys certain legal immunities during the tenure of office. These protections safeguard the dignity and independence of the highest executive authority and prevent unnecessary litigation from obstructing presidential duties. These immunities are laid down in**Article 361 of the Constitution**.**Article 361(1) **states that**the President, shall not be answerable to any court for the exercise and performance of the **powers and duties of his office**or for any act done or purporting to be done by him in the exercise and performance of those powers and duties. This ensures that judicial scrutiny does not interfere with the performance of executive functions. According to**Article 361(2), No criminal proceedings whatsoever shall be instituted or continued against the **President in any court during his term of office**.**

Statement I is incorrect.**Under**Article 361(4), **no civil proceedings in which relief is claimed against the President shall be instituted during his term of office in any court in respect of any act done or purporting to be done by him** in **his personal capacity, **whether before or after he entered upon his office as President except**after giving two **months' notice.**Which simply means that**civil proceedings can be started**against the President during his term of office in respect of his personal acts after giving**two months advance notice. **

Statement II is correct.**Article 361 (3)**states that**no process for the arrest or imprisonment of the President, shall **issue from any court during his term of office**. This immunity ensures that no criminal process can physically restrain the functioning of the President.

II. Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs
III. Cabinet Committee on Investment and Growth
IV. Cabinet Committee on Skill, Employment and Livelihood
V. Cabinet Committee on Accommodation.
How many of the above given cabinet committees are headed by the Prime Minister?
questions.questionText: Consider the following Cabinet Committees in India: I. Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs II. Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs III. Cabinet Committee on Investment and Growth IV. Cabinet Committee on Skill, Employment and Livelihood V. Cabinet Committee on Accommodation. How many of the above given cabinet committees are headed by the Prime Minister?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The Cabinet Committees are extra constitutional bodies, established by the Rules of Business, specifically the Government of India (Transaction of Business) Rules, 1961. These rules are derived from**Article 77(3)**of the Constitution, which grants the President the power to make rules for the more convenient transaction of the business of the Government of India and for the allocation of business among ministers.** Option I is correct:**Cabinet Committee on Economic Affairs **is**headed by the Prime Minister**. This committee reviews economic trends, problems, and prospects, and coordinates all activities requiring policy decisions at the highest level. It also deals with fixation of prices of agricultural produce and prices of essential commodities, industrial licensing policies, and rural development.** Option II is correct:**Cabinet Committee on Political Affairs **is**headed by the Prime Minister.**This committee deals with all policy matters pertaining to domestic and foreign affairs and is often called the "Super Cabinet".** Option III is correct:**Cabinet Committee on Investment and Growth **is**headed by the Prime Minister. **This committee is responsible for reviewing investment and growth policies. It was established in**2019**.** Option IV is correct: Cabinet Committee on Skill, Employment and Livelihood.**is**headed by the Prime Minister. **This committee focuses on employment and skill development policies. It was established in**2019**.** Option V is incorrect:**Cabinet Committee on Accommodation is headed by the union Home minister. **This committee deals with the allotment of government accommodation to various individuals and organizations.
