statementLines: With reference to a Joint State Public Service Commission (JSPSC), which of the following statements is/are correct?
I. It is a statutory body.
II. The Chairman and members of the JSPSC are appointed by the President of India.
III. The members of the JSPSC serve a term of six years or until the age of 65, whichever is earlier.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: With reference to a Joint State Public Service Commission (JSPSC), which of the following statements is/are correct? I. It is a statutory body. II. The Chairman and members of the JSPSC are appointed by the President of India. III. The members of the JSPSC serve a term of six years or until the age of 65, whichever is earlier. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer**.**

Statement I is correct**. While the Constitution permits the creation of a JSPSC under**Article 315(2)**, its**actual **formation takes place through a law enacted by Parliament**, upon resolutions from the concerned states. Hence, the JSPSC is a**statutory body**. Article 315(2) of the Constitution of India states that two or more States may agree that there shall be one Public Service Commission for that group of States, and if a resolution to that effect is passed by the House or, where there are two Houses, by each House of the Legislature of each of those States,**Parliament may by law provide for the **appointment of a Joint State Public Service Commission to serve the needs of those States. **

Statement II is correct**. As per**Article 316(1)**of the Constitution, the**President of India appoints**the Chairman and members of the JSPSC.**

Statement III is incorrect**.**Article 316(2) of the Indian Constitution**states that a member of JSPSC**holds office for **six years or until the age of 62**, whichever is earlier. The age limit of 65 years applies to the members of**UPSC**,**not to **State or Joint Commissions**.

statementLines: Which of the following statements are correct with regard to the Advocate General of a State?
I. S/he shall have the right to take part in the proceedings of the Legislature of the State.
II. S/he shall receive such remuneration as the Legislature of the State may determine by law.
III. A citizen of India who has for at least 10 years held a judicial office in the territory of India becomes eligible for the office of Advocate General of the State.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements are correct with regard to the Advocate General of a State? I. S/he shall have the right to take part in the proceedings of the Legislature of the State. II. S/he shall receive such remuneration as the Legislature of the State may determine by law. III. A citizen of India who has for at least 10 years held a judicial office in the territory of India becomes eligible for the office of Advocate General of the State. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer**. The**Advocate General of the State**, under**Article 165**of the Indian Constitution, is the state's highest law officer. The office serves as a bridge between the state's legal framework and executive functioning. The Advocate General is entrusted with the responsibility of advising the state government on legal matters and representing it in courts. This post mirrors the Attorney General's role at the central level but functions within the jurisdiction of the respective state.**

Statement I is correct**. Article 177 of the Constitution of India states that , Every Minister**and the Advocate-General **for a State shall have the right to speak in, and otherwise to take part in the proceedings of, the Legislative **Assembly of the State or, in the case of a State having a Legislative Council, both Houses,**and to speak in, and otherwise to take part in the proceedings of, any committee of the Legislature of which he may be named a member, but shall not, by virtue of this article, be entitled to vote.**

Statement II is incorrect**. As per Article 165 (3), the Advocate-General shall hold office during the pleasure of the Governor, a**nd shall receive such remuneration as the Governor (not Legislature of State by law) may determine. **

Statement III is correct**. The Governor of each State shall appoint a person who is qualified to be appointed a Judge of a High Court to be Advocate-General for the State. In this context, the qualifications for appointment as a Judge of a High Court are laid down in**Article 217(2**) of the Constitution of India. A**person shall not be qualified for appointment as a Judge of a High Court unless he/she:**1.Is a**citizen of India**, and**2.a) has held a judicial office in the territory of India for at least ten years, or ** b) has been an advocate of a High Court (or of two or more such Courts in succession) for at least ten years.
Hence a citizen of India who has for at least 10 years held a judicial office in the territory of India is eligible for the office of Advocate General of the State.

The doctrine is based on the idea that the State is the ultimate owner of all property within its territory. The description given above most appropriately refers to

questions.questionText: It is a legal principle under which the property of a person who dies without leaving a legal heir or a valid will reverts to the State. The doctrine is based on the idea that the State is the ultimate owner of all property within its territory. The description given above most appropriately refers to which one of the following doctrines?
questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Recently, the Supreme Court of India delivered a significant ruling clarifying the**limited applicability of the Doctrine **of Escheat**under**Section 29 of the Hindu Succession Act, 1955**. The**Doctrine of Escheat**is a common law principle incorporated in**Article 296**of the Indian Constitution. According to this doctrine: When a person**dies intestate**(without leaving a will) and**has no legal heirs**, their property automatically**reverts **to the Government**. The**State**acts as the**ultimate owner**of all property within its territory. The doctrine ensures that no property remains ownerless and that ownership always vests in the sovereign authority**.**

statementLines: With reference to the legislative powers of the Governor of a State in India, consider the following statements:
I. S/he shall from time to time summon the House or each House of the Legislature of the State to meet.
II. S/he can nominate one member of the Anglo-Indian community to the State Legislative Assembly.
III. S/he shall nominate one-sixth of the total number of members of the State Legislative Council in states with Bicameral legislature.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the legislative powers of the Governor of a State in India, consider the following statements: I. S/he shall from time to time summon the House or each House of the Legislature of the State to meet. II. S/he can nominate one member of the Anglo-Indian community to the State Legislative Assembly. III. S/he shall nominate one-sixth of the total number of members of the State Legislative Council in states with Bicameral legislature. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **The Governor, as the constitutional head of a State, plays a significant role in the legislative functioning of the State Legislature.**

Statement I is correct**: Under**Article 174**, the Governor shall from time to time summon the House or each House of the Legislature of the State to meet at such time and place as he thinks fit, but six months shall not intervene between its last sitting in one session and the date appointed for its first sitting in the next session. The Governor may from time to time-a) prorogue the House or either House; and b) dissolve the Legislative Assembly.**

Statement II is incorrect:**The 104th Constitutional Amendment Act of 2019**ended the nomination of Anglo-Indian **members by the Governor of the state to the state legislative assemblies**. The nomination of one Anglo-Indian member to a State Legislative Assembly was previously a provision under**Article 333**of the Constitution, but this provision is no longer in effect.**

Statement III is correct:**The Governor of a state shall**nominate one-sixth of the total members of the State **Legislative Council **from those with special knowledge or practical experience in fields like** literature, science, art, **cooperative movement, and social service**. This provision, as per** Article 171 **of the Indian Constitution, ensures that eminent and experienced individuals are represented in the council.

statementLines: With reference to the procedure for the impeachment of the President of India, consider the following statements:
I. A resolution to impeach the President can be introduced in either House of Parliament.

II. After the House of Parliament in which the resolution to impeach the President was introduced passes the resolution, the other House must constitute a committee of five Supreme Court Judges to investigate charges against the President.
III. The resolution to impeach the President must be passed by both houses of Parliament with a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the respective house.
Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.questionText: With reference to the procedure for the impeachment of the President of India, consider the following statements: I. A resolution to impeach the President can be introduced in either House of Parliament. II. After the House of Parliament in which the resolution to impeach the President was introduced passes the resolution, the other House must constitute a committee of five Supreme Court Judges to investigate charges against the President. III. The resolution to impeach the President must be passed by both houses of Parliament with a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the respective house. Which of the above statements are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 61 provides the procedure for impeachment of the President. The President can be removed from office through a process of impeachment only on the ground of "violation of the Constitution."**

Statement I is correct:**Under**Article 61**, the impeachment of the President may be initiated**in either House of **Parliament**- Lok Sabha or Rajya Sabha. A notice signed by at least one-fourth of the total members of the House initiating the motion must be given, and a minimum of**14 days' notice**is mandatory before the resolution is moved.**

Statement II is incorrect:**There is**no provision**in the Constitution for forming a committee of Supreme Court judges to investigate the charges against the President. Under**Article 61(3) and 61(4)**of the Constitution of India, the investigation is carried out by**the other House of Parliament itself**- that is, if the motion is initiated in the Lok Sabha, the Rajya Sabha investigates the charges, and vice versa. If as a result of the investigation a resolution is passed by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the total membership of the House by which the charge was investigated or caused to be investigated, declaring that the charge preferred against the President has been sustained, such resolution shall have the effect of removing the President from his office as from the date on which the resolution is so passed.**

Statement III is correct:**To begin the process, a notice of motion for impeachment must be submitted to the presiding officer (Speaker or Chairman) of the House. Under**Article 61(1)**of the Constitution of India, the notice must be signed by at least one-fourth of the total number of members of that House. After the 14-day notice period, the motion is taken up in the House for discussion and debate. If**it is approved by a majority of two-thirds of the total **membership, the resolution is sent to the other House for investigation**

questions.explanationMarkdown: **Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **The President of India is required to lay certain reports before Parliament, ensuring accountability and transparency in governance.** Option I is correct: **According to Article 151 (1) of the Constitution of India, the reports of the Comptroller and AuditorGeneral of India relating to the accounts of the Union**shall be submitted to the President,**who shall cause them to be laid before each House of Parliament. These reports form the basis for financial oversight by the Public Accounts Committee (PAC).** Option II is correct:**The Central Vigilance Commission is a statutory body. Under section 14 of the Central Vigilance Commission (CVC) Act, 2003,**It shall be the duty of the Commission to present annually to the President a report as **to the work done by the Commission within six months of the close of the year under report.**On receipt of such a report, the President shall cause the same to be laid before each House of Parliament.** Option III is correct:**The**Union Public Service Commission (UPSC) submits an annual report of its work to the **President of India. **Under Article 323(1) the President then laid this report before each House of Parliament along with a memorandum detailing the reasons for not accepting any of the commission's recommendations.

statementLines: Consider the following statements:
Statement I: The oath of office to the Governor of a state is administered by the President of India.
Statement II: The Governor of a State shall be appointed by the President of India by warrant under his/her hand and seal.
Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements: Statement I: The oath of office to the Governor of a state is administered by the President of India. Statement II: The Governor of a State shall be appointed by the President of India by warrant under his/her hand and seal. Which one of the following is correct in respect of the above statements?

options.a: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct and Statement II explains Statement I.
options.b: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct but Statement II does not explain Statement I.
options.c: Statement I is correct but Statement II is not correct.
options.d: Statement I is not correct but Statement II is correct.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **

Statement I is not correct:**The**oath of office to the Governor is not administered by the President of India**. Under**Article 159**, the oath is administered by the**Chief Justice of the High Court**exercising jurisdiction in relation to the State, or, in his absence, the senior most Judge of that Court available. Under**Article 159, **every Governor and every person discharging the functions of the Governor shall, before entering upon his office, make and subscribe, in the presence of the** Chief Justice of the High Court**exercising jurisdiction in relation to the State, or, in his absence, the senior-most Judge of that Court available, an oath or affirmation.**

Statement II is correct: **Under Article 155, the**Governor of a State shall be appointed by the President by warrant **under his hand and seal. **

statementLines: Which one of the following is not explicitly stated in the Constitution of India but followed as a convention? tenure, the Speaker of the Lower House of the Parliament will officiate as the President.

options.a: The finance minister is to be a Member of the Lower House.

options.c: All the parts of India are to be represented in the Council of Ministers.
options.d: In the event of both the President and the Vice-President demitting office simultaneously before the end of the

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer **While the Constitution outlines the process of forming a government and the basis for a vote of no confidence, it **does not explicitly mention the requirement for the Prime Minister to resign in case of losing the majority. ** Instead, this practice has evolved as a convention in India's parliamentary democracy. This convention is based on the principle of responsible government, where the Prime Minister is accountable to the Parliament, particularly the Lower House where the government needs the support to function effectively.
Knowledge Base: Under President discharge of functions act 1969,in the event of the occurrence of vacancies in the office of the President and Vice President, by reason in each case of death, registration or removal, or otherwise the Chief Justice of India of the Supreme Court of India available shall discharge the functions of the President until a new President elected in accordance with the provisions of the constitution.
The Constitution does not mandate regional quotas for Ministers. However, the convention of including representation from diverse regions in the Council of Ministers is followed to ensure inclusive governance and consider regional interests.

statementLines: Consider the following statements regarding Chief Minister of a state in India:
I. S/he is a member of the Inter-State Council.

II. It shall be the duty of the Chief Minister of each State to communicate to the Governor of the State all decisions of the Council of Ministers relating to the administration of the affairs of the State.
III. S/he shall make rules for the more convenient transaction of the business of the Government of the State, and for the allocation among Ministers of the said business.
Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements regarding Chief Minister of a state in India: I. S/he is a member of the Inter-State Council. II. It shall be the duty of the Chief Minister of each State to communicate to the Governor of the State all decisions of the Council of Ministers relating to the administration of the affairs of the State. III. S/he shall make rules for the more convenient transaction of the business of the Government of the State, and for the allocation among Ministers of the said business. Which of the statements given above is/are correct?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Article 164**states that the**Chief Minister of a State shall be appointed by the Governor of that State**.**

Statement I is correct**: The Chief Minister is a member of both the**Inter-State Council**and the**Governing Council of **NITI Aayog**, both of which are chaired by the Prime Minister.**

Statement II is correct**:**As per Article 167 of the Constitution of India It shall be the duty of the Chief Minister of **each State
- **(a) to communicate to the Governor of the State all decisions of the Council of Ministers relating to the **administration of the affairs of the State **and proposals for legislation; (b) to furnish such information relating to the administration of the affairs of the State and proposals for legislation as the Governor may call for; and (c) if the Governor so requires, to submit for the consideration of the Council of Ministers any matter on which a decision has been taken by a Minister but which has not been considered by the Council.**

Statement III is incorrect**:**The Governor of State (not the Chief Minister of India) **shall make rules for the more convenient transaction of the business of the Government of the State, and for the allocation among Ministers of the said business in so far as it is not business with respect to which the Governor is by or under this Constitution required to act in his discretion.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XI: Relations between the Union and the States
statementLines: Which of the following committees/commissions recommended the abolition of All-India Services in India?
questions.questionText: Which of the following committees/commissions recommended the abolition of All-India Services in India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer **

Option c is correct:**In**1969**, the**Tamil Nadu government**constituted the**P.V. Rajamannar Committee**to examine whether the**Indian Constitution functioned as a truly federal document**and to**recommend amendments that **would ensure the utmost autonomy for the States**. The Committee submitted its**report in 1971**, in which it made several significant recommendations aimed at strengthening**State autonomy**and redefining**Centre-State relations**. It recommended the**constitution of an Inter-State Council**as a**permanent consultative body**to facilitate cooperation and coordination between the Union and the States. It advised that the**Union Government should not enact any new law affecting one or more States without prior **consultation with the Inter-State Council**, thereby ensuring that the federal structure is respected in legislative matters. The Committee proposed the**abolition of All-India Services such as the IAS, IPS, and IFS**, on the ground that these services acted as**instruments of Central control within State administration**and impeded genuine autonomy. Knowledge Base: The**Second Commission on Centre-State Relations**was established by the**Government of India in **2007**under the**chairmanship of Justice Madan Mohan Punchhi**, former Chief Justice of India, to undertake a comprehensive review of the evolving dynamics between the Union and the States. It recommended the creation of**new All-India Services**in fields such as**health, education, engineering, and the **judiciary**, arguing that such services would help strengthen**national standards**, enhance**State-level **administrative capacity**, and ensure**uniform quality**in critical sectors. The Commission also suggested that the**devolution of powers to Panchayats and Municipalities**as**institutions **of self-government**should be**constitutionally defined**through appropriate amendments, to secure**greater **clarity**,**autonomy**, and**functional responsibility**for local bodies. In**1983**, the**Central Government**appointed a**three-member Commission on Centre-State Relations**under the chairmanship of**Justice R.S. Sarkaria**, a retired judge of the Supreme Court. The other members of the Commission were**B. Sivaraman**and**S.R. Sen**. The Commission was tasked with**examining and reviewing the working of the existing arrangements between **the Centre and the States in all spheres**, and to**recommend appropriate changes and measures**to improve the functioning of India's federal system. It strongly recommended the**strengthening of the All India Services**, emphasising that expanding their scope and capacity would enable them to play a**greater role in improving administrative efficiency**, enhancing**policy **implementation**, and promoting**cooperative federalism**across the country. The Hota Committee recommended that**aptitude tests and leadership assessments**should be introduced for the recruitment of civil servants to improve the quality of entrants. It further suggested that**probationers should be allowed one month after the commencement of training to **exercise their final option for services**, thereby giving them time to understand the requirements and nature of different services. The Committee proposed the introduction of**domain-based assignments**for civil servants to promote**specialisation, acquisition of professional skills, and career planning**.

statementLines: Under the provisions of Article 311 of the Constitution of India, in which of the following situations can a member of a civil service of the Union be dismissed/removed without an appropriate inquiry?
I. If the President is satisfied that in the interest of the security of the State it is not expedient to hold such an inquiry.

II. If the authority empowered to dismiss or remove him/her is satisfied that for some reason which should be recorded in writing by that authority, it is not reasonably practicable to hold such inquiry.
III. If that person becomes a member or associated with a political party or joins any organization which takes part in politics.
IV. If that person is dismissed or removed on the ground of conduct which has led to his/her conviction on a criminal charge.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.questionText: Under the provisions of Article 311 of the Constitution of India, in which of the following situations can a member of a civil service of the Union be dismissed/removed without an appropriate inquiry? I. If the President is satisfied that in the interest of the security of the State it is not expedient to hold such an inquiry. II. If the authority empowered to dismiss or remove him/her is satisfied that for some reason which should be recorded in writing by that authority, it is not reasonably practicable to hold such inquiry. III. If that person becomes a member or associated with a political party or joins any organization which takes part in politics. IV. If that person is dismissed or removed on the ground of conduct which has led to his/her conviction on a criminal charge. Select the correct answer using the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **As per Article 311 of the constitution of India, No person who is a member of a civil service of the Union or an all-India service or a civil service of a State or holds a civil post under the Union or a State shall be dismissed or removed by an authority subordinate to that by which he/she was appointed. Also,**No such person as aforesaid shall be dismissed **or removed or reduced in rank except after an inquiry in which he has been informed of the charges against **him/her and given a reasonable opportunity of being heard in respect of those charges. **But this clause shall not apply**
- (a) where a person is dismissed or removed or reduced in rank on the ground of conduct which has led to his conviction on a criminal charge; or **( Option IV is correct.)** (b) where the authority empowered to dismiss or remove a person or to reduce him in rank is satisfied that for some reason, to be recorded by that authority in writing, it is not reasonably practicable to hold such inquiry; or **(Option II is correct.)** (c) **where the Presiden**t or the Governor, as the case may be, **is satisfied that in the interest of the security of the State it is not expedient to hold such inquiry. ( Option I is correct)**

**Option III is incorrect:**Section 5(1) of the All India Services (Conduct) Rules, 1968 under the All India Services Act, 1951 states that "No member of the Service shall be a member of, or be otherwise associated with, any political party or any organization which takes part in politics, nor shall he take part in, or subscribe in aid of, or assist in any other manner, any political movement or political activity. The Central Government after consultation with the Governments of the State concerned have made these rules. So a member of civil services becoming a member or associated with a political party or joining any organization which takes part in politics is not a ground under Article 311 of the Constitution, for the member of a civil service of the Union to be dismissed/removed without an appropriate inquiry.
Rather it is the All India Services (Conduct) Rule.
